title: Is cryptocurrency halal in Islam?
stanceSummary: Yasir Qadhi states that cryptocurrency, especially major established coins like Bitcoin, is permissible in principle because no definitive Shariah prohibition applies. He adds that currency-exchange rules still apply, so crypto trades should be spot/instant and not delayed. He also advises caution and discourages risky speculation with money one cannot afford to lose.
alternateQuestions: Is bitcoin halal or haram in Islam?
What is the Islamic ruling on crypto trading?
Is buying and selling crypto allowed in Islam?
Are Muslims allowed to buy Bitcoin?
transcript:
## Summary of Yasir Qadhi's Position

In a [2019 video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hTl5_w5ZNCg) (starting at [0:26](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hTl5_w5ZNCg&t=26s)), Yasir Qadhi addresses whether cryptocurrency is halal:

- The default fiqh principle for transactions is permissibility unless there is clear proof of prohibition.
- Based on that principle, he says major cryptocurrencies such as Bitcoin are generally permissible.
- He emphasizes that currency-exchange rules apply, so trades should be done on the spot without delayed settlement.
- He cautions Muslims against reckless speculation and advises only risking money one can afford to lose.

---

## Full Lecture Transcript (Cleaned)

### Core Ruling on Cryptocurrency — [0:26](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hTl5_w5ZNCg&t=26s)

The question asks for the Islamic ruling on cryptocurrencies. Yasir Qadhi explains that crypto is a decentralized form of digital commodity/currency and notes that Bitcoin became the most prominent example. He mentions that a fiqh council in North America studied the issue in depth and reached a consensus that no objection rose to the level of making cryptocurrency categorically haram.

He reiterates a foundational legal maxim in transactions: things are permissible unless proven otherwise by clear Shariah evidence. Applying this to crypto, he says the base ruling remains permissibility for major established cryptocurrencies, especially Bitcoin.

### Conditions and Cautions — [5:04](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hTl5_w5ZNCg&t=304s)

He adds that because crypto can function like currency exchange, spot-trade requirements apply: exchanges should be immediate rather than delayed. He also distinguishes between fiqh permissibility and investment advice, warning listeners not to behave foolishly with wealth and not to invest amounts they cannot afford to lose.

He concludes that while crypto is generally permissible in his analysis, each specific token may still require case-by-case review, and Muslims should combine permissibility with prudence.
